questions.scenario: The project is named `devpolaris-orders-prod`. A teammate assumes every resource in it runs in `us-central1`. What is the correct response?

options.text: Project names do not set resource location; inspect each resource's service-specific location.
Every GCP project has exactly one region.
The billing account decides the Cloud Run region.
Labels force all resources into one zone.

questions.explanation: A project can contain resources in many locations. Location belongs to resources and services, not to the project name.

questions.scenario: The Cloud Run service and Cloud SQL instance are accidentally created in different regions for the first production release. Why is this worth fixing or at least reviewing?

options.text: It can add latency, cost, and incident confusion unless the split is a deliberate design choice.
Different regions automatically grant broader IAM permissions.
A database in another region means the project no longer exists.

questions.explanation: Splitting app and data can be valid, but accidental location splits affect latency, cost, and recovery reasoning.

options.text: A region is a geographic area.
A zone is a deployment area inside a region.
Some resources are global, some regional, and some zonal.
Global always means the app's data is copied into every region.

questions.explanation: Global, regional, and zonal are location scopes, but each service still has its own data and behavior model. Do not infer data placement from the word global alone.
